text: The surgeon begins dividing the gastrocolic ligament to achieve inferior mobilization of the transverse colon, using surgical instruments to separate the ligament from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon divides attachments between the gallbladder and liver to facilitate the inferior mobilization of the transverse colon.
The surgical setup provides exposure to the duodenum, with robotic arms positioned for optimal access.

The surgeon initiates the Cattell-Braasch maneuver by dividing the retroperitoneal attachments along the anti-mesenteric border of the duodenum, facilitating exposure and mobilization of the duodenum.
The surgeon uses the tip-up instrument in arm four to retract the duodenum, providing clear exposure for division along the appropriate anatomical planes.
The surgeon uses intraoperative ultrasound to locate the ampulla, providing visual confirmation of its position within the duodenum.
The surgical field displays a stent positioned within the distal common bile duct, visible in the image.
The surgeon places stay sutures on either side of the planned duodenotomy site, preparing for the incision into the duodenum.
The surgeon places a stitch through the duodenum and secures the opposite end to the abdominal wall on both sides of the patient's abdomen, creating counter traction for improved exposure.
The surgeon uses hook electrocautery to create an arduodenotomy, visibly incising the duodenum with precision.
The surgeon successfully identifies the ampulla and the adenoma within the duodenum, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon places a V-lock suture through the superior aspect of the ampulla to deliver the adenoma into the surgical field, enhancing visibility for further manipulation.
The V-lock suture, secured to the abdominal wall, is primarily used to provide retraction during the procedure, maintaining tension on the duodenum for better exposure of the surgical field.
The surgeon delivers the ampulla and duodenum into the surgical field and uses hook electrocautery to initiate resection through the normal-appearing duodenal and ampullary tissue.
The lumen of the bile duct is visualized, and the pancreatic duct is identified at the four to five o'clock position within the surgical field.
The surgeon places the first of two V-lock sutures to re-approximate the distal bile duct to the duodenum, ensuring proper alignment and closure of the surgical site.
The surgeon carefully places a stitch near the pancreatic duct, ensuring it remains unobstructed during the procedure.
The surgeon places the second V-lock suture to approximate the superior bile duct to the duodenum, ensuring proper alignment of these structures.
The surgeon performs circumferential resection of the ampulla and adenoma using electrocautery, progressively freeing the specimen from surrounding tissue.
The specimen is placed into an EndoCatch bag for secure containment and is prepared for transport to pathology.

The surgeon uses the previously placed superior V-lock suture to approximate the distal bile duct to the duodenum in a running fashion, creating a circumferential connection between the two structures.
The surgeon completes the circumferential anastomosis and ties it to the initially placed V-lock suture, securing the connection between the bile duct and the duodenum.
The surgeon removes the biliary stent and proceeds to close the vertical duodenotomy horizontally using previously placed V-lock stay sutures.
The surgeon uses a canal stitch to close the duodenotomy in a single layer, starting from one corner and proceeding to the other.
The surgeon ties the sutures in the center to complete the closure of the duodenotomy, ensuring the approximation of the bile duct to the duodenum.
